role_summary: Hattiesburg attorney; as a child, one of the first Black students to transfer into the city's white schools under freedom-of-choice desegregation; later led the effort to place civil rights historical markers across Forrest County.
ai_reading: Funchess entered the corpus through the 2026-05-31 streamlined-ingestion path as a partial excerpt (the holdings cover roughly the first twenty-three minutes), so the precomputed cosine-neighbor, concept-axis, and influence-graph substrate that drives the AI's-reading panel for older entries has not been generated for entry 139 yet; the numeric placements other pages quote are therefore not asserted here. What the embedding substrate will have to reconcile is a vantage no other Forrest County voice in the collection supplies. The two adjacent Hattiesburg interviews, Ellie Dahmer (entry 36) and Vernon Dahmer, Jr. (entry 121), carry the Dahmer family's adult-organizer account of the same county where the Klan firebombed Vernon Dahmer's home in January 1966; Funchess speaks of that same movement and the Dahmer marker she later helped erect, but from inside a newly desegregated white junior high as a twelve-year-old, narrating lunchroom isolation and choir-room exclusion rather than voter registration or night-rider violence. Her testimony also keeps returning to a single SNCC-and-Freedom-Summer hinge, the moment at age nine when, in her phrasing, the students opened the community's eyes to rights it was not exercising, which positions her account at the seam between the Freedom Summer organizing the corpus documents heavily and the school-desegregation aftermath it records far less often.
biographical_paragraph: Glenda Funchess was born in Hattiesburg, Mississippi, on August 17, 1954, the sixth-youngest of twelve children, and she dates her own arrival to three months after the Brown v. Board of Education decision [src: 2]. Her mother worked as one of the first homemaker aides with the state Department of Human Services and her father was a chef at the University of Southern Mississippi [src: 2]. She traces her political awareness to age nine during Freedom Summer, attending a Freedom School held in 1964 at Mt. Zion Baptist Church, where she was photographed with the folksinger Julius Lester [src: 3]. Recruited with her sister by the NAACP education committee, she transferred under the district's stair-step, freedom-of-choice plan into an affluent white junior high, and her interview gives a firsthand account of a year of sustained hostility there before she returned to the African American Rowan High School [src: 2]. She later earned a law degree in 1984 and served on university faculties between 1979 and 1981 and again from 1992 to 2003 [src: 3]. In November 1999 she donated to Mt. Zion Baptist Church what the resource record describes as the first state historical marker honoring a civil rights site in Mississippi [src: 3]; in 2022 the Mississippi Historical Society gave her an Award of Merit for leading the effort to erect four civil rights markers in Hattiesburg, at Mt. Zion Baptist Church, the Vernon Dahmer home, for Rev. W. D. Ridgeway, and for the Peay v. Cox federal court case [src: 4]. Her oral history was recorded for the Civil Rights History Project by Emilye Crosby at the Hattiesburg Public Library on December 2, 2015, and is held by the Library of Congress as entry 139 [src: 1].

interview_snippets.speaker: Glenda Funchess
interview_snippets.lead_in: Funchess places the start of her political awareness at age nine, during Freedom Summer, when SNCC students came to Hattiesburg:
interview_snippets.quote: But I think the students with SNCC kind of opened their eyes that we had certain rights.

interview_snippets.speaker: Glenda Funchess
interview_snippets.lead_in: On the year she spent after transferring into the white junior high under freedom of choice:
interview_snippets.quote: We were basically, it was a hostile school environment. It went the whole day without anyone talking to us.

interview_snippets.speaker: Glenda Funchess
interview_snippets.lead_in: Describing the lunchroom, where she says no classmate would sit with her for the length of the school year:
interview_snippets.quote: So I was going nine months and no one eating with you. I put my tray down and they would jump up.

interview_snippets.speaker: Glenda Funchess
interview_snippets.lead_in: Recounting a choir rehearsal in which a white classmate began crying before a performance her parents would attend:
interview_snippets.quote: Here she said, my parents are coming. And they're going to see me standing next to Glenda.
